topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun slanted through the dusty window of the hallway, illuminating the peculiar object resting on the welcome mat. It was a thick, cream-colored card, crisp and untouched, bearing no name, no address, just a single, looping script wishing "Happy Birthday." My hand, which had been reaching for the mail, froze mid-air. My cheeks instantly flushed a deep, uncomfortable crimson. I snatched the card up as if it were contaminated, shoving it deep into my pocket, desperate to conceal the evidence of this unexpected, unsolicited attention. Every nerve ending felt suddenly hyper-aware, and I started pacing the small space, muttering apologies to the inanimate objects around me.

[story 2]

I picked up the card with two fingers, my grip tightening until my knuckles were white. The paper felt strangely warm beneath my touch. Who would send this? There was no return address, just a vague, cheerful sentiment. A knot began to tighten in my stomach, a cold, prickly sensation that made my breath hitch. I quickly turned away from the mailbox, pretending to be intensely interested in the peeling paint on the wall. When I finally dared to glance back, the card remained clutched in my hand, a silent, awkward accusation against my own solitary existence. I tucked it away under a stack of unrelated bills, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday, the most mundane of days, when the envelope slipped out of the slot. Inside, nestled amongst junk mail and flyers, was a card. Unsigned. A birthday wish. My heart gave a sudden, frantic thump against my ribs. I stared at the looping handwriting, my mouth slightly agape. A wave of intense self-consciousness washed over me, making my posture stiff and awkward. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to hide, to become invisible. I quickly smoothed the card out, smoothing down my shirt repeatedly, trying to erase the sudden visibility of the situation. I walked directly to the kitchen, needing the solid, unfeeling presence of the counter to anchor me.
